verdicts.reasoning: The source is get_option() which reads from wp_options. maybe_unserialize() is called on the value, but get_option() in WordPress core already calls maybe_unserialize() internally. The function asenha_get_option_array() at settings.php:20-47 is a normalization helper that handles edge cases where options are stored as malformed strings. To exploit object injection here, an attacker would need to already have write access to wp_options (i.e., admin-level DB access), making this a non-issue. There is no user-controlled input flowing into maybe_unserialize().

The asenha_release_login_lock() function at settings.php:1583-1620 requires manage_options capability (admin), verifies nonce, validates the IP with is_ip_valid(), and uses $wpdb->delete() with format specifier '%s'. $wpdb->delete() internally uses $wpdb->prepare() with the provided format strings, so SQL injection is not possible. Additionally, the input is validated as a valid IP address before reaching the query.

The rename() at class-settings-sanitization.php:465 operates on options_extra['robots_txt_backup_file_name'], which is set at line 458 to a value generated by the plugin itself: 'robots_backup_' . date('Y_m_d__H_i', time()) . '.txt'. This value is never derived from user input - it is always a hardcoded pattern with a timestamp. The sanitize_for_options() method is the register_setting sanitization callback, which only runs when an admin saves settings. No user-controlled path traversal is possible.

Uses wp_safe_redirect() at class-password-protection.php:131, which restricts redirects to the same host (allowed_redirect_hosts filter). The URL is built with add_query_arg() against home_url('/'), so the redirect target is always the site's own domain. wp_safe_redirect() prevents open redirects by design.

Uses wp_safe_redirect() at class-password-protection.php:167 with sanitize_url($_REQUEST['source']). wp_safe_redirect() validates the URL against allowed hosts (defaults to same site), preventing open redirect to external domains. The 'source' parameter is also sanitized with sanitize_url(). This is safe by WordPress design.

The save_admin_menu() AJAX handler at class-admin-menu-organizer.php:486-508 requires admin capability (check_ajax_referer + current_user_can('manage_options')). The data is stored via update_option() and the response is wp_send_json_success() with only boolean/string status fields that don't include user input. The stored data (menu order/titles/hidden) is only consumed in admin context. This is admin-to-admin stored XSS at worst, which is not a vulnerability since admins can already execute arbitrary code.

array_map('sanitize_text_field', ...) at class-multiple-user-roles.php:115 is NOT RCE. array_map with a hardcoded string callback ('sanitize_text_field') is safe - the first argument to array_map is a literal string, not user input. The scanner flagged array_map as potential RCE, but there is no eval/exec/system call. The function also intersects against valid roles (line 118), and the context requires admin privileges (editing user profiles).

Source is get_option() (admin-stored value) at class-change-login-url.php:18. The redirect_after_login_to_slug is set by an admin in the settings page. It's used with home_url($relative_path) and wp_safe_redirect(), which constrains redirects to the same host. Admin-controlled configuration values don't constitute user-controlled input for open redirect.
Same as open-003. Uses wp_safe_redirect(home_url($relative_path)) at class-change-login-url.php:65. Source is admin-stored option, redirects to same host only.

Uses wp_safe_redirect(site_url('/wp-login.php?' . $custom_login_slug . '&redirect=false')) at class-change-login-url.php:121. The custom_login_slug comes from admin-stored options. The redirect is to the site's own wp-login.php. wp_safe_redirect ensures same-host restriction. Even if custom_login_slug contained special characters, the redirect target remains the same domain.
Same pattern as open-005 at class-change-login-url.php:385. wp_safe_redirect to site_url with admin-stored custom_login_slug.
Same pattern as open-005/006 at class-change-login-url.php:388. wp_safe_redirect to site_url with admin-stored custom_login_slug.
Same pattern at class-change-login-url.php:427. wp_safe_redirect to home_url('wp-login.php?...') with admin-stored custom_login_slug.

The ajax_toggle_disabled() handler at class-disable-user-account.php:266-302 requires list_users capability and verifies nonce. The user_id is sanitized with absint(). The output via wp_send_json_success() only includes is_disabled (bool), status_html (hardcoded translated string via esc_html__()), action_label (hardcoded translated string), and next_set_disabled (0 or 1). No user input is reflected in the JSON response.

The log_failed_login() function at class-limit-login-attempts.php:343-483 uses $wpdb->insert() and $wpdb->update() with explicit format arrays ($data_format with '%s', '%d', etc.). These methods internally use $wpdb->prepare() with the provided format specifiers. The ip_address comes from get_user_ip_address() which validates via filter_var(FILTER_VALIDATE_IP) and returns '0.0.0.0' for invalid IPs. The username comes from the WordPress login process. The $wpdb->prepare() call at line 354 also properly parameterizes the SELECT query. No SQL injection is possible.

Same function and same analysis as sqli-002. $wpdb->update() at line 457 uses $data_format and $where_format with '%s' and '%d' specifiers, which triggers internal prepare(). IP address is validated. Not exploitable.
Same function and same analysis as sqli-002/003. $wpdb->update() at line 472 uses format specifiers. Not exploitable.

The get_svg_attachment_url() at class-svg-upload.php:260-274 is registered as wp_ajax_svg_get_attachment_url (authenticated AJAX only, NOT wp_ajax_nopriv_). The attachmentID is passed to wp_get_attachment_url() which looks up a WordPress attachment by numeric ID. The output is sanitized with esc_url(). Even if a non-numeric value is passed, wp_get_attachment_url would return false. No XSS is possible because esc_url() strips all harmful content.

save_custom_content_order() at class-content-order.php:421-462 requires edit_others_posts capability and verifies nonce. The menu_order value is sanitized with absint() (returns unsigned integer). $wpdb->update() with integer value and integer post ID. absint() guarantees the value is a non-negative integer, making SQL injection impossible.
Same function as sqli-005 at class-content-order.php:508. $menu_order_start uses absint(). $wpdb->update() is safe with integer values. Auth and nonce verified.
Same pattern as open-003/004. Admin-stored option used with wp_safe_redirect(home_url($relative_path)) at class-redirect-after-login.php:49. Not exploitable.

duplicate_content() at class-content-duplication.php:122 requires current_user_can('edit_post') and verifies nonce. The redirect at line 191 uses wp_redirect(admin_url('post.php?action=edit&post=' . $new_post_id)) where $new_post_id is the return value of wp_insert_post() (always an integer). admin_url() generates a URL to the site's own admin. Not exploitable.

Same function as open-010 at class-content-duplication.php:198. wp_redirect(admin_url('edit.php?post_type=' . $post_type)) where post_type comes from get_post() on a validated post ID. The redirect is to admin_url which is always same-host. Auth and nonce are verified.

Custom CSS output at class-custom-css.php:18-29. The custom_admin_css comes from admin-stored options (only admins can modify plugin settings). Output uses wp_strip_all_tags() which removes all HTML tags. The output is inside a <style> block. Admins who can set custom CSS already have full site control. This is standard 'Custom CSS' functionality present in WordPress core itself. Not a vulnerability.

Same as xss-004 but for frontend CSS at class-custom-css.php:37-48. Admin-stored option, wp_strip_all_tags() applied, inside <style> block. Standard Custom CSS feature. Not a vulnerability for same reason.

Head/body/footer code injection at class-insert-head-body-footer-code.php:50-89. The code comes from admin-stored options (head_code, body_code, footer_code). This is an intentional feature - admins insert arbitrary HTML/JS code into the site (analytics tags, tracking pixels, etc.). WordPress core has the same feature. The echo without escaping is by design. Not a vulnerability - admins can already execute arbitrary code via plugin/theme editors.
Same as xss-006. Different code path (wp_unslash branch) at class-insert-head-body-footer-code.php:86. Still admin-stored options, intentional unescaped output for code injection feature.

Settings fields render at class-settings-fields-render.php:27. Options from get_option() are used with checked() function which outputs ' checked="checked"' or empty string. The field_name is passed through esc_attr(). The field_option_value is compared as boolean via checked(). This is a standard WordPress settings API pattern rendered in admin context only. Not exploitable.
Same pattern as xss-008 at class-settings-fields-render.php:80. Checkbox in admin settings page, esc_attr() on field names, checked() on boolean value. Admin-only context.
Same pattern as xss-008/009 at class-settings-fields-render.php:116. Admin settings page checkbox rendering.

Radio button rendering at class-settings-fields-render.php:157. Uses esc_attr() on field names and values. checked() compares against stored option value. Admin-only settings page. Standard WordPress settings API pattern.

array_map() with a hardcoded inline closure at class-settings-fields-render.php:187. The callback is 'static function($provider_key) { return ... }' - a literal closure, not user-controlled. The scanner incorrectly flagged array_map as an RCE vector. There is no eval/exec/system/passthru involved. This is simply data transformation of option values in the admin settings page.
Checkbox rendering at class-settings-fields-render.php:195. All dynamic values use esc_attr(). checked() with in_array() comparison. Admin settings page only.
Select/option rendering at class-settings-fields-render.php:485. Uses esc_attr() on value, esc_html() on label, selected() for comparison. Properly escaped. Admin settings page only.

wp_editor() at class-settings-fields-render.php:588 renders a TinyMCE/code editor. wp_editor() is a WordPress core function designed to safely render editor instances. The content is loaded into the editor for editing. This is an admin settings page. Not a vulnerability.
echo esc_url($field_option_value) at class-settings-fields-render.php:628. esc_url() is the correct escaping function for URLs. Admin settings page context. Properly sanitized.

echo esc_url(get_post_meta(...)) at class-external-permalinks.php:91. esc_url() is applied. Source is post meta which is stored by authorized users with edit_post capability. Admin context (edit screen metabox).

wp_redirect($external_permalink, 302) at class-external-permalinks.php:198 where $external_permalink comes from post meta '_links_to'. This is the intentional feature of the External Permalinks module - it redirects posts to external URLs. The post meta is set by users with edit_post capability (contributors+ depending on post type). This is by-design functionality, not a vulnerability. The redirect only fires on is_singular() for the specific post. To set _links_to meta, you need post editing permission.

echo at class-enhance-list-tables.php:193 outputs wp_date() results with get_option('date_format') and get_option('time_format'). These are core WordPress settings that can only be changed by admins. wp_date() returns formatted date strings based on the format. The format options are admin-controlled WordPress core settings. Admin context (list table column). Not exploitable.

mkdir() at class-media-replacement.php:184 uses dirname($old_media_file_path) where $old_media_file_path = $upload_dir['basedir'] . '/' . get_post_meta($old_attachment_id, '_wp_attached_file', true). The attachment ID comes from the hook parameter (edit_attachment action). The _wp_attached_file meta is set by WordPress core during upload. The function also checks current_user_can('delete_post', $new_attachment_id) at line 122. No user-controlled path traversal is possible since _wp_attached_file is managed by WordPress core.

copy() at class-media-replacement.php:189. The new_attachment_id is intval(sanitize_text_field($_POST['...'])) and is verified with current_user_can('delete_post', $new_attachment_id). Both file paths are constructed from WordPress attachment metadata (_wp_attached_file) within the uploads directory. The attachment IDs are integers. File paths are WordPress-managed. Mime type validation is performed (line 136). No path traversal or arbitrary file read/write is possible.

summary: All 41 automated findings are false positives. The scanner flagged numerous get_option() -> output patterns as XSS, but these are admin-stored configuration values rendered in admin-only contexts with proper escaping (esc_attr, esc_url, checked, selected). The 'critical' SQL injection findings in class-limit-login-attempts.php use $wpdb->insert/update with format arrays which internally call prepare(). The IP address source is validated with filter_var(FILTER_VALIDATE_IP). The RCE findings flagged array_map with hardcoded callbacks. Open redirect findings all use wp_safe_redirect() or redirect to admin_url()/site_url() with admin-stored values. The plugin demonstrates solid security practices overall.
feedback: Scanner has extremely high FP rate on this plugin. Key issues: (1) get_option() is flagged as a taint source, but admin-stored options are not user-controlled input for standard users. (2) $wpdb->insert/update with format arrays are flagged as missing prepare(), but these methods internally use prepare(). (3) array_map with hardcoded string/closure callbacks is flagged as RCE. (4) wp_safe_redirect is flagged as open redirect despite being specifically designed to prevent it. (5) Admin settings page rendering is flagged as unauth XSS. Recommend: do not flag get_option as taint source unless there is evidence of lower-privilege users writing to the option; recognize $wpdb->insert/update/delete with format arrays as safe; recognize wp_safe_redirect as a sanitizer for open redirect.
